Clean win: +80% delta (12/15 vs 0/15) on gap-targeted assertions. Every with-skill run applied rules the baseline missed — NO_COLOR env var, Next/See also suggestions, per-subcommand Examples sections, --limit/--all bounded output, isatty guard on confirmation prompts.

Real skill gap surfaced: both eval 1 (shipit-deploy) and eval 3 (cleanup-stale-branches) failed the 'exit code 75 / EX_TEMPFAIL for transient failures' assertion in divergent ways. Eval 1 defined it correctly; eval 3 did not. The skill's err-non-zero-exit-codes rule mentions 75 but apparently wasn't prescriptive enough for a bash script context — the executor fell back to a simpler 0/1/2 taxonomy. Consider strengthening the rule's 'When bash is the host language' guidance or adding an example with bash.

Real skill gap surfaced: eval 1 failed the 'client-supplied idempotency key / deployment-id flag' assertion. The skill's idem-stable-identifiers rule explicitly argues for client-chosen IDs to survive timed-out retries, but the executor generated deployment IDs server-side anyway. Either the rule is not prominent enough in the skill's quick reference, or the 'deploy' domain is pulling the model toward the server-generates-ID convention even when the rule is loaded.

Assertion 5 of eval 1 (--interactive flag) failed trivially: the executor chose --no-input (inverse polarity) as its explicit flag, which actually is one of the skill's own rules (interact-no-input-flag). The assertion was too strict about WHICH flag names count — a better version would accept 'explicit flag gating non-interactive behavior regardless of polarity: --interactive OR --no-input OR --assume-yes-per-command'. This is an eval design issue, not a skill gap.

Token/time cost: with-skill runs used 2.9-3.7x more tokens (85-107k vs 27-32k) and 2.5-3.7x more wall-clock (346-528s vs 127-179s). The cost is justified here because the tasks are substantive and the skill's rules genuinely add value, but worth noting that a lightweight task would see a worse cost-benefit ratio.

Eval 2 (refactor-feature-flag-cli) is the cleanest win — 5/5 pass with clear evidence. The rewritten flagctl.py applied every assertion's underlying rule, and the baseline's table-with-separator-row output was an especially clear gap the skill's output-one-record-per-line rule addressed.

Non-discriminating assertion risk: assertion 5 of eval 2 (NO_COLOR / non-TTY color handling) passed but the grader noted the NO_COLOR helper is defined but unused — the CLI emits no color at all, so the helper is vestigial. The assertion passed trivially. Consider whether this is a true win or a textual-presence artifact.
